title: Digital Marketing
text: Below are selected projects that reflect my experience in digital marketing strategy, campaign analysis, SEO research, and data-driven decision making. These projects combine creative marketing ideas with analytics tools to better understand customer behavior, improve engagement, and support business goals.

title: Digital Marketing
section: ODI Grips AI Caption Strategy Project
text:
ODI Grips AI Caption Strategy Project
For this graduate research project, I analyzed how AI-generated social media captions performed under different prompting strategies. Using historical Instagram data from ODI Grips, I evaluated how structured prompts influenced caption quality, engagement potential, and brand alignment.
The project combined marketing strategy with analytics by comparing caption performance patterns, testing audience engagement metrics, and presenting findings through visual reports and statistical analysis.
Skills Demonstrated:

Content Strategy: Evaluated caption structure, tone, and audience engagement.
Marketing Analytics: Analyzed Instagram performance metrics using R.
Consumer Engagement Research: Studied how prompt structure influenced perceived quality.
Data Visualization: Built visual reports and presentation-ready findings in Quarto.

title: Digital Marketing
text:
Marketing Dashboard & Reporting Projects
Throughout my graduate coursework, I created multiple dashboards and marketing reports designed for executive audiences. These projects focused on translating complex datasets into clear business insights using R, Quarto, Tableau, and visualization tools.
I emphasized clean reporting design, storytelling through data, and actionable recommendations rather than simply presenting numbers.
Skills Demonstrated:

Marketing Reporting: Developed stakeholder-focused reports and presentations.
Data Storytelling: Simplified technical findings into business insights.
Visualization Design: Built clean and interactive dashboards.
Strategic Communication: Presented findings in an executive-friendly format.

title: Contact Us
text: Template

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
2.1 Client Context
The client is the MSDM (Master of Science in Data Management) program, which operates an informational and lead-generation website built on Odoo. The website serves prospective students, current applicants, and program stakeholders.
The program’s marketing team needs to understand:

Where is traffic coming from? — Which channels and geographies are driving visits?
Who is visiting? — Device usage, behavior, and engagement depth.
Which channels actually drive conversions? — Beyond surface-level traffic, which channels contribute most along the full user journey?
Where should marketing budget be focused? — Given limited resources, which channels have the highest return?

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
section: 2.2 Why Standard Reporting Is Not Enough
text:
2.2 Why Standard Reporting Is Not Enough
The default view in Google Analytics uses last-touch attribution: the final channel a user visited before converting receives 100% of the credit. This model is simple but systematically misleading for programs like MSDM where:

Users typically discover the program through social media or organic search, then return via direct or email links to convert.
Upper-funnel channels (Paid Social, Organic) never receive credit under last-touch even if they were the original discovery source.
Budget decisions made on last-touch data tend to over-invest in closing channels and under-invest in awareness channels.

This report addresses that gap by combining descriptive reporting (from the GA4 snapshot) with a Markov chain attribution model that distributes credit across all touchpoints in the user journey.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
3.1 Source and Pipeline


Show code
library(arrow)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(scales)
library(lubridate)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(forcats)
library(fs)
library(here)
library(readr)
library(gt)
library(knitr)
library(ggrepel)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.width = 9, fig.height = 5)

# ── Paths ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DATA_DIR     <- here::here("data")
PARQUET_DIR  <- fs::path(DATA_DIR, "msdm_snapshot", "events_flat")
PARQUET_FILE <- fs::path(PARQUET_DIR, "events_snapshot.parquet")
CSV_PATH     <- fs::path(DATA_DIR, "events_snapshot.csv")

# ── Build parquet if needed ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
if (!fs::file_exists(PARQUET_FILE)) {
  fs::dir_create(PARQUET_DIR)
  arrow::write_parquet(
    readr::read_csv(CSV_PATH, show_col_types = FALSE),
    PARQUET_FILE
  )
}

# ── Load dataset ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ds <- arrow::open_dataset(PARQUET_DIR) |> dplyr::collect()


BigQuery source: odoo-487003 / analytics_523126945
Pipeline:
BigQuery (GA4 raw tables)
   ↓  SQL extract (flat event schema)
events_snapshot.csv
   ↓  Apache Arrow / write_parquet()
events_snapshot.parquet
   ↓  R (arrow, dplyr, ggplot2)
Analysis + Dashboard + Report
All analysis in this report runs from a local Parquet snapshot. No live BigQuery connection is required after the initial export. This ensures the pipeline is stable and reproducible.

3.3 Key Definitions







Term
Definition

Counted by user_pseudo_id (anonymous; resets across devices/browsers)


Traffic Medium
First-touch medium from traffic_medium; (none) recoded as direct


Page Views
Events where event_name == "page_view"

Not available in this snapshot; page-view sequences used as proxy for attribution


Session
Not reconstructed; no timestamp-based sessionization applied

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
4.3 Traffic by Channel


Show code
traffic_summary |>
  mutate(medium_clean = fct_reorder(medium_clean, page_views)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = medium_clean, y = page_views, fill = medium_clean)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(page_views)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Page Views",
       title = "Page Views by Traffic Medium",
       caption = "Filtered to page_view events; (none) recoded as direct") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())





Page views by traffic medium. Direct traffic dominates, which may reflect either strong brand recognition or incomplete UTM tagging on campaigns.






Show code
traffic_summary |>
  mutate(pct = round(page_views / sum(page_views) * 100, 1)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(medium_clean = "Medium", page_views = "Page Views", pct = "% Share") |>
  fmt_number(columns = page_views, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(columns = pct, decimals = 1) |>
  data_color(columns = page_views, palette = "Greens") |>
  tab_options(table.width = pct(100))








Medium
Page Views
% Share




direct
43
72.9


referral
14
23.7


(not set)
2
3.4





Traffic medium summary with percentage share.


Interpretation: Direct traffic accounts for the largest share of page views. However, a high proportion of Direct traffic is a common indicator of incomplete UTM tagging across campaigns — users who click untagged email or social links will appear as “direct” in GA4. This is addressed directly in the attribution section below.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
4.4 Device Usage


Show code
p1 <- device_summary |>
  ggplot(aes(x = "", y = pct_events, fill = device_category)) +
  geom_col(width = 1, color = "white") +
  coord_polar(theta = "y") +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(device_category, "\n", round(pct_events, 1), "%")),
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5), size = 4, fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Event Share by Device", fill = "Device") +
  theme_void()

p2 <- device_summary |>
  mutate(device_category = fct_reorder(device_category, n_unique_users)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = device_category, y = n_unique_users, fill = device_category)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(n_unique_users)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.2))) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Unique Users", title = "Unique Users by Device") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())

gridExtra::grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)





Device share of events (left) and unique users (right). Desktop dominates, typical for graduate program research behavior.




Desktop dominates both event share and unique users, consistent with graduate program research behavior (users comparing programs, reading requirements, completing applications). Mobile unique users are non-trivial, however, and represent an audience that merits continued UX investment.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
5.1 Motivation
Last-touch attribution — the default in most analytics platforms — assigns 100% of conversion credit to the final channel before a conversion event. This is convenient but misleading for multi-step user journeys. For the MSDM website, a typical journey might look like:
[Paid Social ad] → [Organic search] → [Email link] → [Direct visit / convert]
Under last-touch, Direct receives all the credit. Paid Social and Organic receive none, even though they initiated and nurtured the journey.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
6.1 1. Increase Investment in Paid Social
Recommendation: Increase Paid Social budget allocation, treating it explicitly as an upper-funnel awareness channel.
Evidence: Paid Social receives only 6% under Last-Touch but 14.2% under Markov — a 2.4× increase. Its First-Touch share is 23%, the highest of any channel, confirming it is the primary journey initiator for converting users. It systematically loses credit to Email and Direct as those channels close the conversion.
Confidence: Moderate. The attribution dataset shows a consistent pattern, but Paid Social impressions from walled-garden platforms (Meta, LinkedIn) are not fully captured in click data, so the true contribution may be even higher.
Action: Increase Paid Social spend, add UTM parameters to all ad links, and track Paid Social as a top-of-funnel metric (reach, journey initiation) rather than a last-click conversion metric.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
section: 6.2 2. Maintain Email as a Conversion Channel
text:
6.2 2. Maintain Email as a Conversion Channel
Recommendation: Keep Email campaigns strong for bottom-of-funnel re-engagement (application deadline reminders, event invites, follow-ups).
Evidence: Email leads Last-Touch attribution (28%) and First-Touch (24%), and ranks 4th in Markov (16.3%). It is effective at both initiating and closing journeys — the most versatile channel in the mix.
Confidence: High. Email’s strong performance is consistent across all four attribution models.
Action: Segment email lists by funnel stage. Use upper-funnel emails for prospects who arrived via Paid Social or Organic; use conversion-focused emails for users already engaged with the program.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
section: 6.3 3. Re-evaluate Direct as a Standalone Channel
text:
6.3 3. Re-evaluate Direct as a Standalone Channel
Recommendation: Do not treat Direct traffic as an independent channel to invest in — it is primarily the closing step of journeys begun elsewhere.
Evidence: Direct receives 21% under Last-Touch but only 19.3% under Markov, and its First-Touch share is just 8% — the lowest of any channel. This confirms Direct rarely initiates journeys; it mainly captures users returning to convert after prior touchpoints.
Confidence: High. The First-Touch / Markov discrepancy is diagnostic of a closing-step channel.
Action: Focus investment on the channels (Paid Social, Organic, Email) that generate the journeys that Direct closes. Improve UTM tagging to reduce mis-attribution of untagged campaign traffic into Direct.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
section: 6.4 4. Invest in Referral as a Mid-Funnel Channel
text:
6.4 4. Invest in Referral as a Mid-Funnel Channel
Recommendation: Treat Referral as a mid-funnel nurturing channel and explore partnerships that generate referral traffic.
Evidence: Referral’s Markov share (13.9%) is notably higher than its Last-Touch share (10%) and its First-Touch share (5%), indicating it plays a meaningful role in the middle of converting journeys — keeping users in the funnel after initial discovery.
Confidence: Moderate. Referral is a heterogeneous category (partner sites, listings, directories) and its sources are not broken out in this dataset.
Action: Identify the top referral sources, prioritize partnerships with high-Markov-value referrers, and ensure all referral links are UTM-tagged for channel-level attribution.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
section: 6.5 5. Rebalance Upper vs. Lower Funnel Spend
text:
6.5 5. Rebalance Upper vs. Lower Funnel Spend
Recommendation: Shift some budget from lower-funnel channels (Email, Direct-associated) toward upper-funnel channels (Paid Social, Organic content, Referral partnerships).
Evidence: The Markov model consistently re-weights credit toward channels that appear early in journeys (Paid Social: +8.2pp vs Last-Touch; Referral: +3.9pp) and away from channels that appear late (Email: −11.7pp; Direct: −1.7pp).
Confidence: Moderate. Requires cost data (not currently available) to calculate ROI-adjusted reallocation.
Action: Add cost data to the pipeline and re-run attribution with weighted ROI metrics before committing to specific budget shifts.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
6.6 5. Strengthen UTM Tagging
Recommendation: Audit and enforce UTM tagging across all outbound links (email campaigns, social posts, paid ads).
Evidence: A large proportion of Direct traffic is likely mis-attributed traffic from untagged campaigns. Improving tagging would immediately improve the accuracy of both last-touch and Markov models.
Confidence: High. This is a data quality fix with no downside risk.
Action: Implement a UTM naming convention standard and verify all active campaign links before next semester’s recruitment cycle.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
7.1 Data Constraints







Constraint
Impact




8 snapshot days only
Trends are directional; no seasonality, no weekly patterns


user_id largely missing
Cross-device stitching not possible; one user on two devices = two pseudo-users


No conversion events
Attribution is modeled from page-view sequences as proxy; not actual conversions


Nested GA4 fields excluded
event_params and ecommerce arrays were flattened out; no page-level or product-level detail


No session reconstruction
Without timestamp-based sessionization, session-level metrics (bounce rate, session duration) are unavailable

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
7.2 Attribution-Specific Constraints







Constraint
Impact




Walled-garden data gaps
Paid Social impressions (Meta, LinkedIn) are not fully recorded in GA4 click data; Markov may undercount Paid Social


Small journey paths
Markov chains require sufficient multi-touchpoint sequences; small datasets increase variance in removal effect estimates


No cost or ROI data
Attribution percentages cannot be converted to budget recommendations without spend data


First-touch vs. last-touch vs. Markov
No single model is “correct”; Markov is the most theoretically sound for multi-step journeys but not immune to data quality issues

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
7.3 What This Report Cannot Claim
This report cannot claim:

That Paid Social caused conversions — only that it appears in more conversion paths than last-touch suggests.
That the trends observed over 8 days are representative of the full academic year.
That the Markov attribution percentages are stable — with more data, they would likely shift.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics — Final Report
text:
8.1 How to Reproduce This Report

Clone the repository:
git clone https://github.com/hsujeffrey/ibm6530-group4-project
cd ibm6530-group4-project
Ensure data is available: Place events_snapshot.csv in data/. The Parquet file will be auto-built on first render.
Install R packages:
install.packages(c(
  "arrow", "dplyr", "ggplot2", "scales", "lubridate",
  "stringr", "tidyr", "forcats", "fs", "here",
  "readr", "gt", "knitr", "gridExtra", "ggrepel"
))
Render the report:
quarto render IBM6530_Final_Group4_MSDM_Odoo.qmd

└── presentation.qmd                      # Deliverable 3 — Revealjs Presentation

title: ODI Grips Social Media Analysis
text:
Overview
This project analyzed ODI Grips social media engagement data to better understand how content structure, captions, hashtags, and calls-to-action may influence audience interaction and engagement performance. The analysis explored trends across Instagram content to identify patterns that could support stronger content strategy and digital engagement.




Learning Outcomes

Analyze engagement trends across social media content.

Examine how caption structure and hashtags relate to engagement performance.

Identify audience interaction patterns through engagement metrics.

Evaluate differences across content and caption types.

Translate engagement findings into content strategy recommendations.




Key Skills Gained

Social media analytics

Engagement metric analysis

Data wrangling in R

Content performance evaluation

Data visualization and reporting

title: Dashboards & Reporting
text:
Overview
This work focused on building dashboards and visual reports to communicate marketing and website analytics findings more clearly and effectively. Using tools such as R, Quarto, Tableau, GA4, BigQuery, and Looker Studio, the dashboards were designed to simplify complex datasets and support data-informed decision-making.




Learning Outcomes

Organize and visualize marketing and website analytics data.

Build dashboards to monitor performance metrics and trends.

Translate complex data into more accessible visual insights.

Support stakeholder communication through reporting.

Develop data-informed recommendations from dashboard findings.




Key Skills Gained

Dashboard development

Data visualization

Reporting and storytelling

Data wrangling in R

Marketing analytics tools

title: SEO & Search Visibility Analysis
text:
Overview
This project evaluated keyword opportunities, competitor visibility, and website content performance to better understand areas for SEO improvement. The analysis explored search trends, content gaps, and visibility patterns to support stronger digital marketing and content strategy decisions.




Learning Outcomes

Identify keyword opportunities through search and competitor research.

Analyze website visibility and search performance trends.

Evaluate competitor content and digital presence.

Recognize content gaps and SEO improvement opportunities.

Develop data-informed recommendations for digital strategy.




Key Skills Gained

SEO analysis

Keyword research

Competitive benchmarking

Data interpretation and reporting

Digital marketing strategy

Work Experience

Intake Specialist & Marketing / Legal Assistant, Cha Cha Cha Law
Oct. 2025 – current

Serve as a primary point of contact for prospective and existing clients regarding lemon law and personal injury matters.
Conduct client intake, gather case information, and organize supporting documents including repair orders and purchase agreements.
Assist with legal and administrative operations by preparing client communications, coordinating case updates, and scheduling attorney calls.
Support marketing initiatives through client outreach, digital content coordination, and brand communication across online platforms.




Brand Community Engagement Specialist, Center for Customer Insights & Digital Marketing (CCIDM)
Mar. 2025 – Oct. 2025

Supported the Center Director in developing and executing engagement initiatives that increased student awareness and participation in events and workshops.
Created and scheduled social media and email marketing campaigns to improve audience reach and program visibility.
Collaborated with faculty, students, and campus organizations to coordinate logistics and promote digital marketing programs.
Assisted with community outreach projects involving branding, student engagement, and digital communication strategies.
Utilized tools including Canva, Google Workspace, HubSpot, and social media platforms for campaign coordination and content management.




Barista, 6xs Coffee DTLA
Jul. 2024 – Oct. 2025

Maintained professional and efficient customer service in a fast-paced café environment with high daily order volume.
Managed multiple beverage and food orders while ensuring accuracy, quality, and customer satisfaction.
Collaborated with team members to maintain workflow efficiency during peak business hours.
Assisted with customer communication, delivery coordination, and daily operational support.
Developed strong multitasking, communication, and problem-solving skills in a customer-facing environment.




English Teacher, Dosol Elementary School
Mar. 2022 – Feb. 2024

Taught English to over 700 elementary students weekly, improving classroom participation and communication skills.
Designed interactive lesson plans and educational materials in collaboration with Korean co-teachers.
Organized English Camps and group activities that encouraged teamwork, confidence, and cultural exchange.
Incorporated digital learning materials and multimedia tools to improve student engagement and learning outcomes.
Strengthened leadership, public speaking, and cross-cultural communication skills through classroom instruction and program coordination.

title: Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Overview
This project analyzed website traffic and engagement data for the MSDM Odoo website using GA4, BigQuery, and dashboard reporting tools. The analysis focused on understanding user behavior, traffic acquisition sources, engagement patterns, and overall website performance to identify opportunities for improving user experience and digital visibility.




Learning Outcomes

Analyze website traffic and user engagement metrics.

Identify trends in acquisition channels and user behavior.

Evaluate engagement performance across website pages.

Communicate analytics findings through dashboards and reports.

Develop data-informed recommendations for website improvement.




Key Skills Gained

GA4 and BigQuery analysis

Website analytics reporting

Dashboard development

Data wrangling in R

Data visualization and storytelling

title: AI Caption Quality Research
text:
Overview
This research project examined how historically informed prompting influenced the quality of AI-generated social media captions for ODI Grips. Captions generated from basic prompts and historically informed prompts were evaluated across dimensions such as brand alignment, clarity, engagement potential, persuasiveness, and originality to better understand how structured prompting may improve AI-generated marketing content.




Learning Outcomes

Compare AI-generated captions across different prompting conditions.

Evaluate content quality using structured human rating methods.

Analyze how historical engagement insights can inform prompt design.

Interpret statistical findings related to content quality performance.

Translate research findings into practical content strategy recommendations.




Key Skills Gained

AI-generated content evaluation

Marketing research methods

Statistical analysis in R

Data visualization and reporting

Social media content analysis

title: Professional Summary
MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Business Problem


Client: MSDM Program — Odoo Website
Core Questions:

Where is traffic coming from?
Who is visiting & on what device?
Which channels drive conversions?
Where should budget be focused?


Why it matters:
The MSDM program needs to understand its digital presence to make smarter decisions about outreach and marketing investment.
Standard last-touch reporting tells an incomplete story — this project adds multi-touch attribution.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:

Source

GA4 event data from BigQuery
Project: odoo-487003
Dataset: analytics_523126945

Coverage

8 snapshot days
Feb 9 – Apr 3, 2026
~7 week span

events_snapshot.csv
   ↓  Apache Arrow
events_snapshot.parquet
   ↓  R / Quarto
Analysis + Dashboard
Key fields: event name, traffic medium, device, geo, user pseudo ID

Where Is Traffic Coming From?



Organic and Direct together dominate — the MSDM site relies heavily on brand awareness and search visibility.

Desktop dominates — typical for program research. But mobile unique users warrant continued UX investment.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text: Attribution: Last-Touch vs Markov

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
What the Models Tell Us (2/2)



Channel
Insight




Email
Strong closer & initiator


Direct
Closing step, not a true driver


Organic
Stable across all models


Paid Search
Consistent contributor


Paid Social
⚠️ Undervalued by last-touch


Referral
Mid-funnel role undervalued





Paid Social: 6% last-touch → 23% first-touch → 14% Markov. It starts journeys but gets no closing credit.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Recommendations (1/2)

Invest more in Paid Social — only 6% last-touch, but 23% first-touch and 14% Markov. It initiates journeys but gets almost no closing credit.
Keep Email strong — top in both last-touch (28%) and first-touch (24%). Best for bottom-of-funnel re-engagement.
Don’t over-budget Direct — 21% last-touch but only 8% first-touch. It’s the final step, not a true driver.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Recommendations (2/2)

Invest in Referral partnerships — Markov (14%) > Last-Touch (10%). Meaningful mid-funnel role that position-based models miss.
Rebalance upper vs lower funnel — shift some spend toward Paid Social and Referral while maintaining Email efficiency.

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Limitations


Data Constraints

Only 8 snapshot days — trends are directional, not conclusive
user_id largely missing — no cross-device stitching
Nested GA4 fields excluded (event_params, ecommerce)
No session-level metrics


Attribution Constraints

Direct traffic may mask untagged channels
Paid Social impressions not fully captured (walled garden)
Small dataset — results may shift with more data
No cost or ROI data to validate recommendations

title: MSDM Odoo Website Analytics
text:
Next Steps

Strengthen UTM tagging across all campaigns to reduce Direct traffic inflation
Expand date coverage — 8 days is not enough for reliable trend analysis
Add cost data — combine attribution results with spend to calculate channel ROI
Test Paid Social increase — run an experiment to validate the Markov model’s findings
Unpack event_params — enables page-level analysis and deeper engagement metrics

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
My work in marketing analytics connects data, digital strategy, and storytelling. Through my MS in Digital Marketing coursework and hands-on projects, I’ve worked with website analytics, campaign data, social media engagement, SEO research, attribution modeling, and dashboard reporting.
A lot of my work focuses on making data easier to understand. I enjoy taking messy or complex datasets and turning them into clear insights that can help explain what is happening, why it matters, and what a team could do next.

title: Marketing Analytics
text:
What You’ll Find

Odoo Website Analytics: Analyzing GA4 and BigQuery data to understand website traffic, user behavior, engagement, and conversion patterns.
ODI Grips Social Media Analysis: Studying Instagram engagement, caption structure, hashtags, CTAs, and content types to identify patterns in content performance.
AI Caption Quality Research: Comparing basic prompts with historically informed prompts to evaluate how AI-generated captions can improve in brand alignment, clarity, engagement potential, and persuasiveness.
SEO & Search Visibility: Reviewing keyword opportunities, competitor visibility, and website content to support stronger digital marketing strategy.
Dashboards & Reporting: Building dashboards and Quarto reports using tools like R, GA4, BigQuery, Tableau, Looker Studio, and SQL.


These projects reflect my interest in using analytics not just to report numbers, but to support better marketing decisions. My goal is to communicate insights in a way that feels practical, visual, and easy for others to understand.
